introSection:
G-Force (formerly named The Device, and The Sling Shot) is a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Newton's Attic in Lexington, Kentucky, USA.

It operates during special events and can be rented for birthday parties.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The Device was built in spring 2002 by nine pre-engineering students at West Jessamine High School, along with Newton's Attic founder Bill Cloyd, as one of its "Demo-Active Devices", which are large pieces of equipment built by students of partnered schools to demonstrate principles of science and engineering. It began life as a traveling roller coaster, visiting multiple schools as Newton's Attic did not yet have a permanent facility.

In 2012, Newton's Attic purchased a former tractor dealership and turned it into a physical location for the organization, and the coaster was moved there shortly after. Around this time, the coaster was also renamed The Sling Shot.

From 2013 to 2016, the coaster attended the Louisville Mini Maker Faire, an annual single-day event. By 2015, the coaster had been renamed to G-Force.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
G-Force is entirely human-powered and requires no electricity. The rider, or multiple non-riders, pull a rope, which pulls the catch car back, which continually stretches a bungee cord resting on the car via a hook. Once the car is pulled to their desired distance, or to the end of the launch track, the car is manually released from the catch car with a lever. The car is launched to a max speed of before the bungee cord loses contact with it, after which it goes up the  tall vertical rollback. After rolling back down, the car bounces off the bungee cord again, repeating the layout until the car is stopped.

Bill Cloyd was the creator and primary designer of the coaster, as well as handling important parts of fabrication. Students at West Jessamine High School handled the bulk of fabrication as well as assisting with design.

sections.title: Appearances
sections.content:
* East Jessamine High School Project Graduation, Spring 2002
 * West Jessamine High School, Spring 2002
 * Southside Center for Applied Technology, Spring 2002
 * University of Kentucky Engineering Day, February 22, 2003
 * Tates Creek High School Science Fair, March 2003
 * Eastern Kentucky University, May 20, 2003
 * Western Kentucky University, May 22, 2003
 * Louisville Mini Maker Faire, September 28, 2013
 * Louisville Mini Maker Faire, September 27, 2014
 * Louisville Mini Maker Faire, September 19, 2015
 * Louisville Mini Maker Faire, September 10, 2016

fullText:
G-Force (formerly named The Device, and The Sling Shot) is a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Newton's Attic in Lexington, Kentucky, USA.

It operates during special events and can be rented for birthday parties.

The Device was built in spring 2002 by nine pre-engineering students at West Jessamine High School, along with Newton's Attic founder Bill Cloyd, as one of its "Demo-Active Devices", which are large pieces of equipment built by students of partnered schools to demonstrate principles of science and engineering. It began life as a traveling roller coaster, visiting multiple schools as Newton's Attic did not yet have a permanent facility.

In 2012, Newton's Attic purchased a former tractor dealership and turned it into a physical location for the organization, and the coaster was moved there shortly after. Around this time, the coaster was also renamed The Sling Shot.

From 2013 to 2016, the coaster attended the Louisville Mini Maker Faire, an annual single-day event. By 2015, the coaster had been renamed to G-Force.

G-Force is entirely human-powered and requires no electricity. The rider, or multiple non-riders, pull a rope, which pulls the catch car back, which continually stretches a bungee cord resting on the car via a hook. Once the car is pulled to their desired distance, or to the end of the launch track, the car is manually released from the catch car with a lever. The car is launched to a max speed of before the bungee cord loses contact with it, after which it goes up the  tall vertical rollback. After rolling back down, the car bounces off the bungee cord again, repeating the layout until the car is stopped.

Bill Cloyd was the creator and primary designer of the coaster, as well as handling important parts of fabrication. Students at West Jessamine High School handled the bulk of fabrication as well as assisting with design.
